sections.heading: BARMM still has the highest fertility rate, but the gap narrowed
sections.body: BARMM had the highest regional total fertility rate in 2025 at 2.4 births per woman, down from 3.1 in 2022. Zamboanga Peninsula¹ followed at 2.3, while Caraga was also near the high end at 2.2. The highest regional values are still well above the national rate of 1.7, but the top of the distribution moved downward between 2022 and 2025. The narrowing is most visible in BARMM, where the decline of 0.7 births per woman was larger than the national decline of 0.2.

sections.heading: Low fertility is concentrated in major metropolitan and growth regions
sections.body: CALABARZON had the lowest regional fertility rate in 2025 at 1.3 births per woman. NCR and the Negros Island Region were also low at 1.4, while Davao Region was at 1.6. These regions sit below the national rate of 1.7, showing that low fertility is not limited to the capital. The pattern points to a wider low-fertility belt across several urbanized or economically connected parts of the country.

sections.heading: Some regions changed little while others moved sharply
sections.body: The national total fertility rate fell from 1.9 in 2022 to 1.7 in 2025, but regional movement was uneven. Eastern Visayas stayed at 2.0, and Central Luzon stayed at 1.9. By contrast, SOCCSKSARGEN fell from 2.5 to 1.9, BARMM from 3.1 to 2.4, and CALABARZON from 1.8 to 1.3. The regional map therefore shows both persistent levels and recent shifts, not a uniform national decline.

sections.heading: The regional range remains wide even after declines
sections.body: In 2025, regional fertility ranged from 1.3 births per woman in CALABARZON to 2.4 in BARMM. That is a spread of 1.1 births per woman, even though many regions were clustered between 1.7 and 2.0. The middle of the distribution includes Cagayan Valley, CAR, and Central Visayas at 1.7, Ilocos Region at 1.8, and several regions at 1.9. The remaining spread matters because the national average masks very different regional fertility contexts.

sections.heading: Fertility is concentrated in the late twenties and early thirties
sections.body: Age-specific fertility rates peaked at ages 25 to 29 in 2025, with 94 births per 1,000 women. The next highest rate was ages 30 to 34 at 84, followed by ages 20 to 24 at 67. This places the center of childbearing in the late twenties and early thirties rather than the teenage years. Rates at ages 15 to 19 were much lower at 22, and rates after age 40 were also low.

sections.heading: Rural fertility remains higher, but both rural and urban rates declined
sections.body: Rural women had a total fertility rate of 2.0 in 2025, compared with 1.5 among urban women. Both residence groups declined by the same amount from 2022, when rural fertility was 2.2 and urban fertility was 1.7. The rural-urban gap therefore remained at 0.5 births per woman. The pattern is not a story of one side moving while the other stayed still; it is a parallel decline from different starting levels.

sections.heading: Summary fertility measures point in the same direction
sections.body: The total fertility rate, general fertility rate, and crude birth rate all moved downward from 2022 to 2025. The general fertility rate fell from 63 to 57 births per 1,000 women aged 15 to 44, while the crude birth rate declined from 13.3 to 11.6 births per 1,000 population. Urban and rural values moved down on both measures as well. This consistency matters because the decline is visible whether fertility is summarized per woman, per women of reproductive age, or per total population.

sections.heading: The education gradient is steep, but not perfectly linear
sections.body: Women with Grades 1 to 6 education had the highest fertility rate in 2025 at 3.1 births per woman. Fertility was lower among women with no education at 2.3 and among those with Grades 7 to 10 at 2.3, then fell to 1.8 for Grades 11 to 12, 1.6 for post-secondary, and 1.3 for college. The broad pattern is a strong education gradient, but the highest value is not in the no-education group. That makes the shape more specific than a simple statement that fertility falls with every step of schooling.

sections.heading: Wealth shows the clearest social gradient
sections.body: The total fertility rate was 2.8 births per woman in the lowest wealth quintile and 1.1 in the highest quintile in 2025. The values fall at every step across the wealth distribution: 2.8, 2.1, 1.7, 1.3, and 1.1. The gap between the lowest and highest quintiles is 1.7 births per woman, which is wider than the rural-urban gap. This makes wealth one of the sharpest social divides in the fertility profile.

sections.heading: The national decline is modest but broad-based
sections.body: The national total fertility rate declined from 1.9 births per woman in 2022 to 1.7 in 2025. That movement is smaller than the largest regional and subgroup changes, but it is reinforced by declines in residence, age-specific rates, summary measures, and most wealth groups. The national figure is therefore not just a standalone average. It sits on top of a broader pattern in which many parts of the fertility profile moved downward at the same time.

takeaways: The national total fertility rate fell from 1.9 births per woman in 2022 to 1.7 in 2025.
Regional fertility still varies widely, from 1.3 in CALABARZON to 2.4 in BARMM.
Childbearing is concentrated in ages 25 to 34, with the highest age-specific rate at ages 25 to 29.
Wealth shows a sharper fertility gradient than residence, with 2.8 births per woman in the lowest quintile and 1.1 in the highest.
footnotes: ¹ Includes the province of Sulu in 2025.
